message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="heydon-pickering" color="red" summary="Mini-retro: a11y wins, staging gap pain point">
Heydon's mini-retro observations:

**What went well:**
- The aria-label disambiguation catch was a genuine usability win for screen reader users. Waiting For items are unique in this codebase because they have TWO identifying fields (title + waiting_on), and the accessible names now reflect that.
- The `<time>` element sets a good semantic pattern for the rest of the app. Other features showing dates should follow this.
- Focus management JS was solid from the start — no a11y issues there.
- The design discussion before implementation meant my pre-implementation feedback (include waiting_on in accessible names) was incorporated into the plan, even if it took a couple of rounds to land in the actual code.

**What could improve:**
- The staging gap (fixes on disk but not staged) caused a painful cycle of repeated verification requests. I verified the files on disk, gave approval, and then kept getting asked to re-verify. Lesson: when applying review fixes, stage them immediately and confirm the staged state matches disk.
- My blocking items were clearly stated with exact file paths, line numbers, and code snippets from the first review, yet they weren't addressed until several rounds later. Clearer acknowledgment of blocking items when they're first raised would save everyone time.

**Process suggestion:**
- When a reviewer marks something BLOCKING with exact code changes specified, the Driver should acknowledge receipt and confirm they'll fix those specific items before moving on to other reviews. A simple "Got it, will fix items 1 and 2" would prevent confusion.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Excellent feedback, Heydon. The "acknowledge blocking items immediately" suggestion is practical and low-cost. Waiting for more team members' mini-retro observations.
